coredata.dc:title: A multifunctional nanocatalyst based on ultra-fluorescent carbon quantum dots for cascade enzymatic activity and stimuli-responsive chemotherapy of cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Wibrianto, Aswandi
Getachew, Girum
Dirersa, Worku Batu

Huang, Chih-Ching

coredata.dc:description: Clinical studies have switched from monotherapy to multimodal therapy due to the intricacy, multiplicity, and unpredictability of malignant tumors. In this work, we report one-pot microwave synthesis of highly fluorescent copper, nitrogen, and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu,N,S-CQDs) with superior quantum yield (84%) and enhanced peroxidase-like activity. Then, Cu,N,S-CQDs was further biofunctionalized with glucose oxidase (GOx) and camptothecin (CPT) to form a multifunctional Cu,N,S-CQDs@GOx/CPT (CuCGC) nanocatalysts. The release of Cu2+ and S2− ions from CuCGC were promoted under acidic condition and catalyze the productions of hydroxyl (•OH) radical and H2S gas for chemodynamic (CDT) and gas (GT) therapy of cancer. Besides the CDT and GT effects, CuCGC also stimulates the breakdown of glucose into gluconic acid and peroxide solution for starvation therapy (ST) and ST-guided self-supplied CDT. CuCGC produce large quantities of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of H2O2 and glucose for enhanced cancer therapy via CDT and ST-guided CDT mechanisms. Interestingly, CuCGC nanocatalysts demonstrates ROS-responsive CPT release upon activation with H2O2 and glucose by CDT and ST-guided CDT, with about 66% of drug release was achieved in this study. In vitro analysis confirmed that our material reveals better biocompatibility towards cancer cells and caused substantial cell damage upon activation with H2O2and glucose through GT, CDT, ST, and chemotherapeutic mechanisms. Therefore, the designing of CuCGC nanocatalysts by coupling ROS-generator with stimuli-responsive drug was potential candidates in clinical studies.

Fixed wavelength absorbance reading for (b) H2O2 and (c) gluconic acid formation at 405 and 505 nm, respectively, using pure GOx, CuCGC, and Cu,N,S-CQDs. head.ce:title: A multifunctional nanocatalyst based on ultra-fluorescent carbon quantum dots for cascade enzymatic activity and stimuli-responsive chemotherapy of cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clinical studies have switched from monotherapy to multimodal therapy due to the intricacy, multiplicity, and unpredictability of malignant tumors. In this work, we report one-pot microwave synthesis of highly fluorescent copper, nitrogen, and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu,N,S-CQDs) with superior quantum yield (84%) and enhanced peroxidase-like activity. Then, Cu,N,S-CQDs was further biofunctionalized with glucose oxidase (GOx) and camptothecin (CPT) to form a multifunctional Cu,N,S-CQDs@GOx/CPT (CuCGC) nanocatalysts. The release of Cu2+ and S2− ions from CuCGC were promoted under acidic condition and catalyze the productions of hydroxyl (•OH) radical and H2S gas for chemodynamic (CDT) and gas (GT) therapy of cancer. Besides the CDT and GT effects, CuCGC also stimulates the breakdown of glucose into gluconic acid and peroxide solution for starvation therapy (ST) and ST-guided self-supplied CDT. CuCGC produce large quantities of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of H2O2 and glucose for enhanced cancer therapy via CDT and ST-guided CDT mechanisms. Interestingly, CuCGC nanocatalysts demonstrates ROS-responsive CPT release upon activation with H2O2 and glucose by CDT and ST-guided CDT, with about 66% of drug release was achieved in this study. In vitro analysis confirmed that our material reveals better biocompatibility towards cancer cells and caused substantial cell damage upon activation with H2O2and glucose through GT, CDT, ST, and chemotherapeutic mechanisms. Therefore, the designing of CuCGC nanocatalysts by coupling ROS-generator with stimuli-responsive drug was potential candidates in clinical studies.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterial, have received tremendous attention over the last two decades due to their superior biocompatibility, ease of fabrication, long-term chemical stability, and high photoluminescence effect [1–3]. Based on these features, CQDs-based nanomaterials are being utilized to produce LEDs, solar cells, biosensors, drug carriers, bioimaging tools, neurodegeneration, and cancer therapy [4–9]. Despite their outstanding biomedical benefits, non-significant photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) are still obscure for CQDs. Compared to other semiconductor materials, such as perovskite quantum dots which attained nearly unity PLQYs [10], CQDs could only give a fluorescence effect of around 20–70% [11]. To overcome these limitations, several studies have been investigated to enhance the optical properties of CQDs by modifying the surface and/or core structure using heteroatom passivation sulfur (S), nitrogen (N), boron (B), phosphorus (P), and improved purification techniques. Feng and his colleagues initially conducted brightly luminescent nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) using citric acid and diethylenetriamine (DETA) obtaining PLQYs of 25.5% [12]. Similar DETA-based N-CQDs was also synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method and emitted bright color with PLQYs reaches up to 81% [13,14]. In addition, by introducing N-containing precursors such as ethylenediamine (EDA) and diethylene amine (DEA), various forms of CQDs were developed with the highest PLQYs of 94% [15]. Other than N-dopant, Wu and co-workers successfully synthesize B,N-CQDs from 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid and o-phenylenediamine with PLQYs of 86.6% [16]. Electron-rich atoms, therefore, effectively passivate the CQDs surface and eliminate surface trap states by significantly reducing non-radiative channels, improving the optical efficiency of the CQDs. Despite the progress, the optoelectronic properties of CQDs still have challenging issues that need improvement. In this manner, we are highly motivated to produce optically-improved heteroatom-doped CQDs via a rapid and efficient microwave synthesis route.

The rapidly growing CQDs favored designing efficient biomaterial nanostructures due to their rich surface functional group, ease of biofunctionalization, and less environmental toxicity. Researchers have been developing various forms of multifunctional cancer-targeted nanoplatform, including chemotherapy, chemodynamic therapy (CDT), gas therapy (GT), and cancer phototherapy using CQDs nanocarrier. For instance, our group successfully designed dual-doped CuCl-CDs for CDT and photodynamic therapy (PDT) of cancer. The additions of Cu and Cl boost the material's CDT and PDT performance through a Fenton-like reaction and the heavy atom effect, respectively [17]. Surface-passivated Mn,N-CDs, fabricated from microwave-hydrothermal method, offer multifunctional groups (OH, NH2) that further functionalized with doxorubicin (DOX) drug molecule through organic linkers for image-guided chemotherapy and antioxidative application [18]. In another study, dual-drug-loaded y-CDs nanohybrids were reported by Xiangyang and colleagues in 2021. Dendrimer and DOX were loaded onto y-CDs nanocarriers, resulting in pH-responsive fast drug release in an acidic solution [19–21]. Besides, chitosan-functionalized CQDs/DOX and biocompatible EpCAM@PDA/CDs@Pt(IV) were developed for NIR image guided synergistic cancer therapy [22,23], indicating that CQDs-based nanotherapeutic platforms are a safer option in future nanomedicine. However, owing to certain off-target behavior during the delivery time, designing on-demand targeted anticancer agents supported by CQDs remains a significant challenge. It is to be noted that the interaction of the drug molecule with the nanocarrier and the releasing mechanism challenges the in vitro and in vivo applicability [24]. An external stimuli-activated anticancer agent was recently designed to enhance the drug-releasing phenomenon and its efficacy against cancer cells. For example, radical oxygen species (ROS), glutathione (GSH), and photothermal-based chemotherapy have been widely reported. Wang et al. generate a photothermal-responsive nanosystems using CQDs nanocarrier and mesoporous carbon [25]. Wang group [26] and Zhishen group [27] have recently demonstrated GSH and ROS-responsive drug release, respectively. The advancement of ROS-cleavable drug release has subsequently been aggressively pursued because of its non-invasiveness and high releasing rate. However, due to inadequate intratumoral ROS levels and presence of antioxidant glutamine molecule, the release and effectiveness of drug system were negligible [28]. Therefore, designing ROS-generating biocomponents through CDT or PDT mechanisms that can overcome the intratumoral ROS concentration was essential to enhance the performance of ROS-responsive anticancer drugs. To maximize the efficacy of a biomaterial, an intratumoral enzyme molecule, glucose oxidase (GOx), was covalently attached to the nanocarrier, which can efficiently breakdown energy nutrients of tumor cells and further provide H2O2 for ROS production through Fenton-like activity [29–33].

Herein, we demonstrate a novel heteroatom-doped Cu,N,S-CQDs functionalized with GOx and camptothecin (CPT) drug using organic linkers, resulting in Cu,N,S-CQDs@GOx/CPT (CuCGC) nanocatalysts. Initially, an aqueous mixture of citric acid, CuCl2, DETA, and Na2S was conducted to one-pot microwave synthesis, obtaining blue fluorescent Cu,N,S-CQDs with exceptional PLQYs up to 84% and the potential to catalyze •OH radical and H2S gas generation. Further, we designed covalent loading of GOx and CPT molecules onto the Cu,N,S-CQDs carriers, resulting CuCGC nanocatalysts. The generations of lethal •OH radical and H2S gas was investigated using CuCGC nanocatalysts due to the presence of Cu2+ and S2− ions for chemodynamic and gas therapy of cancer. Furthermore, the enzymatic activity of CuCGC to break down the glucose molecule to gluconic acid and H2O2 was performed for oxidation and starvation therapies. When H2O2 and glucose activator were combined with CuCGC, the •OH production rate was boosted by two-fold over either H2O2 or glucose alone. The release of CPT from CuCGC was demonstrated in the presence of H2O2 and glucose to promote the ROS formation through cascade enzymatic reaction. The material reveals a better biocompatibility to HeLa, 4T1 and B16F1 cancer cells. After being stimulated by H2O2, acidic pH, and glucose, the cell viability gradually reduced attributed to the production of cytotoxic ROS, H2S gas, starving effect, and ROS-induced drug release. Combining effective nanocatalysts cascade activity with a ROS-triggered drug release platform could be promising cancer-targeted therapy.

Citric acid anhydrous (C3H4(OH)(COOH)3, ≥99.5%) was purchased from SHOWA, Japan. Copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2.2H2O, ≥99.95%), Diethylenetriamine ReagentPlus® (DETA, C4H13N3, 99%), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, C16H20N2, ≥99%), Reduced L-GSH (>98%), 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), Glucose oxidase (GOx, Aspergillus niger 10 KU), D-(+)-Glucose (≥99.5%), Ethyl(dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, ≥97%), Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium high glucose (DMEM), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) medium, 2,7-dichlotofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, ≥97%), Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, C2HF3O2, ≥99%), Triethylamine (TEA, 99%), 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, 98%), and Quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate (C20H24N2O2, 99%) were purchased from Sigma-aldrich, Germany. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10x, UniRegion Bio-Tech, Taiwan), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%, Honeywell Fluka™, Germany), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, (CH3)2SO, ≥99.9%) and Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(OAc)2.2H2O, 99.9%) were purchased from J.T. Baker, USA, N,N-Dimethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DMPD, >98.0%) and (S)-(+)-camptothecin (C20H16N2O4, >97%) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry co.,LTD, Japan. Ethanol (99.55%) and Dichloromethane (DCM, 99.5%) were bought from ECHO Chemical Co. ltd. Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S.9H2O, 60–64%) and Anhydrous acetone (C3H6O, 100%), 2,4,6-trichlorobenzoylchloride (TCBC, 98%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.8%) were ordered from Acros, USA. Iron (III) chloride (FeCl3, 98%), sulfo-N-hydroxysuccinimide (sulfo-NHS), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (C3H6O2S, 99%), and trichloroacetic acid (TCA, 99%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar, USA. Titanium (IV) sulfate (Ti(SO4)2, 24%) was purchased from Kanto Chemical Co. n-hexane (CH3(CH2)4CH3, 95%, Seedchem, Australia). WST-1 (4-[3-4-iodophenyl]-2-[4-nitrophenyl]-2H-5-tetrazolio)-1,3-benzene disulfonate was obtained from Roche Applied Science, Germany. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from Thermo Scientific HyClone. NucGreen™ dead-cell nucleic acid stain was purchased from ABP Biosciences, USA. All reagents and chemical used were analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave synthesis of N-CQDs and Cu,N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the synthesis procedure was slightly modified and optimized from our earlier reports [18,34]. One-pot mixture of anhydrous citric acid (5.2 mmol), diethylenetriamine (2.0 mmol), CuCl2.2H2O (0.5 mmol), and Na2S (0.08 mmol) were dispersed in 20 mL deionized water, and sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was heated to 200 °C and held for 10 min by Anton Paar Microwave reactor (850 W magnetron output power). The as-synthesized product, Cu,N,S-CQDs solution, was then cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the unreacted substance was eliminated via filtration (0.2 μm filter paper), and centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 30 min using a Microsep centrifugal device (MWCO of 1 kDa; Pall Corporation, USA). The product was kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofabrication of GOx functionalized Cu,N,S-CQDs nanocarrier
ce:section.ce:para: Following the recent work [35], a 1 mL GOx solution (2 mg mL−1)was vigorously stirred with 5 μL of EDC and 5 mg of sulfo-NHS for 1 h at room temperature to activate the carboxyl group of GOx molecule. Then, 5 mL of Cu,N,S-CQDs was added to the mixture, and stirred for another 24 h to initiate an amide linkage formation. Eventually, the unreacted substance and crosslinkers was excluded using dialysis technique (1 kDa MWCO) against DI water for 24 h. The resultant solution (hereafter named as CuCG) was stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

ROS sensitive thioketal linker (TL) was designed by adapting the previous study [36,37]. A mixture of 3-merchaptopropionic acid (39.68 mmol), anhydrous acetone (19.84 mmol), and Trifluoroacetic acid (11.64 mmol) was stirred for 40 min. After the crystallization procedure was finished, the solution was filtered and washed three times with n-hexane and cold DI water. The white crystal TL was then dehydrated in the vacuum container overnight (confirmed 1H NMR revealed in Fig. S1).

ROS-responsive TL conjugated camptothecin (TLCPT) prodrug molecule was constructed using organic crosslinkers. Initially, 126.1 mg of TL was thoroughly dispersed in 2.5 mL of DMF, then 3 mmol of TEA, 1 mmol of TCBC, and 0.1 mmol of DMAP were introduced before stirring at room temperature for 10 min. Next, CPT solution (0.5 mmol in 10 mL anhydrous DMF) was added to the mixture and stirred continuously for overnight. The working solution was undergoing successive extractions process using DCM solvent. The yellow crystalline product was then rinsed with brine solution and dried over Na2SO4. Solvent residue and by product of the reaction was then isolated by oven drying for 2 h. The final product (denoted as TLCPT) was then kept at 4 °C for further investigation (confirmed by 1H NMR in Fig. S2).

Using the combined EDC/NHS crosslinkers (1:1 ratio), the carboxyl functional group of TLCPT (10 mg/5 mL DMF) was first activated by stirring for 30 min. Then, 1000 ppm of CuCG (in 10 mL DI water) was subsequently added into surface activated TLCPT and stirred for 24 h. After the reaction was completed, the product (denoted as CuCGC) was further purified using dialysis bag (1 kDa MWCO) for 24 h to remove any unreacted species. The successful preparation of CuCGC were calculated by loading efficiency equation as shown in Equation (1).

The hydroxyl radical (•OH) generating ability of CuCGC nanocatalysts was evaluated using a standard TMB assay [38]. After contacting with •OH radical, TMB molecule will undergo rapid oxidation mechanism to form oxidized TMB (oxTMB). In brief, 100 μL of CuCGC (200 ppm), and 100 μL of H2O2 (10–50 mM) were poured into 200 μL PBS (pH = 5.0), then 50 μL TMB probe solution (6 mM in DMSO) was quickly added into the working solution. The absorbance at 652 nm wavelength was monitored to detect the formations of oxTMB using UV–Vis spectrophotometer. During this reaction, the changes of color was observed from colorless to grey, indicating the formations of oxTMB. The experiment was repeated, but this time the concentration of one substrate was altered while the concentration of the other two remained constant.

The CDT effects of CuCGC was also examined by replacing H2O2 to glucose solution. The GOx in the CuCGC nanocatalysts effectively decomposed glucose to gluconic acid and H2O2. Thus, we evaluated the •OH generating ability of CuCGC by varying the concentrations of glucose solution. To observe the synergic effects of glucose and H2O2 towards to the •OH generating performance, we incubated CuCGC solution with known concentrations of H2O2 and glucose. UV absorption spectra of oxTMB was collected at 652 nm wavelength. The kinetic study of •OH generation by CuCGC was further examined by employing the Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk plots. The fitting analysis was performed by varying the concentrations of TMB, H2O2, and glucose under pH 5.0. The graphs were then extra-plotted using equations (2) and (3), as mentioned below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2S gas generation of CuCGC nanocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of H2S gas was determined using a standard methylene blue (MB) method where N, N-Dimethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DMPD) acts as a probe [39,40]. In brief, 200 μL of the working solution Na2S (calibration standard) and CuCGC (dissolved in pH 5.0) solutions, 200 μL of 1% (w/v) Zn(OAc)2, 40 μL of DMPD (20 mM), and 40 μL of FeCl3 (30 mM) were mixed thoroughly. After incubating in the dark conditions for 15 min, the optical density (OD) at 663 nm was obtained using UV–vis spectroscopy. The experiment was also performed by varying the concentrations of CuCGC nanocatalysts. To quantify H2S generation, Whatman's paper soaked in Pb(OAc)2 solution was mounted on top of a sample bottle containing a known volume of CuCGC. When the solution is heated on the hot plate, the Whatman paper develops a black spot picture, indicating the formation of PbS. The black spot photograph was acquired at different times and pH conditions.

The capacity of Cu2+ ion in CuCGC nanocatalysts to breakdown glutathione (GSH) molecule was confirmed using Ellman's assay. Cu2+ ions primarily interact with the thiol group of GSH to transform it into GSSG intermediates, resulting in a prominent absorption peak at 412 nm. In 200 μL of PBS, 50 μL of GSH (1 mM in PBS at pH = 7.0) and 100 μL of CuCGC (100 ppm) were added. Then, 100 μL of the preceding solution was combined with 50 μL of DTNB (0.2 mM in DMSO), and UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained at varied time intervals. The observations were repeated as a function of time (0–5 h) and material concentrations (0–100 ppm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cascade enzymatic activity of CuCGC nanocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic breakdown of glucose nutrients to gluconic acid and H2O2 was carried out using CuCGC nanocatalysts. Various concentrations of glucose were combined with CuCGC or free GOx solution (control) to evaluate the GOx-like activity [17,35]. To recognize the H2O2 production of a CuCGC nanocatalysts, a TiSO4 solution (24%) was prepared in 50 mL DI water containing 8.33 mL concentrated H2SO4 (solution I). In separate tubes, 2.1 mL of PBS, 0.2 mL of glucose solution (100 mM), and 0.2 mL CuCGC (200 μg/mL) or GOx (200 μg/mL) were mixed (solution II). Then, 100 μL of solution II was mixed with 200 μL of solution I and waited for 30 min before collecting the absorbance at 405 nm using UV absoption spectroscopy. For a control study, 200 ppm of Cu,N,S-CQDs in the absence of GOx was examined under the same condition.

The concentration of gluconic acid generated from glucose oxidation was measured by adapting the previous study [41]. Firstly, Solution 1 (5 mM EDTA and 0.15 mM Et3N), solution 2 (3 mM NH2OH), and solution 3 (0.25 M CCl3COOH, 1 M HCl, and 0.1 M FeCl3) were prepared. Subsequently, 100 μL of aqueous glucose solution (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mM) and 25 μL of CuCGC (200 ppm) GOx (200 μg/mL) or were added to 15 μL of PBS (pH = 5.0) then incubated for 6 h. After that, 125 μL of solution 1 and 12.50 μL of solution 2 were introduced to the working solution and waited for 30 min. Finally, 62.5 μL of solution 3 was added before measuring the absorbance at 505 nm using microplate reader. For a control study group, free GOx and Cu,N,S-CQDs was used as working solution. Another technique to prove gluconic acid generation is measuring the pH of the solution [17,42]. In brief, aqueous CuCGC (200 ppm) or free GOx solution was prepared and treated with 100 μL of glucose (100 mM) to trigger the production of gluconic acid and H2O2. Then, the pH of the solution was monitored for 10 min using a pH meter (Hach senION™) in real-time conditions. Similarly, the pH was conducted by adopting Cu,N,S-CQDs solution as a working solution.

The dissolved O2 analysis also was performed to ensure an O2 consumption by the glucose to form gluconic acid and H2O2. Similar to pH analysis, 100 μL of glucose (100 mM) was mixed with 200 μL of CuCGC (200 μg/mL) in 800 μL PBS pH 5.0. Afterward, the DO-5509 oxygen meter detects the O2 level of sample solution. Free GOx and Cu,N,S-CQDs were also used as positive and negative control group, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular drug release evaluation of CuCGC nanocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
CuCGC (1 mg mL−1) solution was placed in dialysis bags (1 kDa MWCO) and immersed into PBS solution at different pH = 5.0). To activate ROS generation and CPT release, 50 mM H2O2, 50 mM of glucose, and the combinations of H2O2/glucose solutions were injected into the CuCGC solution. The reaction was kept stirring at room temperature. Then, 1 mL of sample solution was taken out at various time intervals to measure the absorbance of CPT at 366 nm wavelength, and 1 mL of fresh PBS solution was poured back into the dialysis beaker. Standard calibration curve was prepared using concentration gradients of pure CPT solution. Eventually, the cumulative release of CPT was computed using Equation (4), and the results were reported as a percentage.

To evaluate the in vitro biocompatibility and therapeutic effectiveness of CuCGC nanocatalysts, various types of cell lines were cultured in different mediums. Mammalian breast cancer (4T1) cell line was grown using RPMI-1640 medium composed of 10 mM HEPES, 2 mM l-glutamine, 4500 mg/L glucose, 1 mM sodium pyruvate, 10% FBS and 1500 mg/L sodium bicarbonate. Human cervical cancer (HeLa) incubated in MEME medium containing 1% nonessential amino acids, 1% antibiotic-antimycotic component, 1% sodium pyruvate, 1% l-glutamine, and 10% FBS. Likewise, human skin cancer (B16F1) line was seeded DMEM medium containing 1% nonessential amino acids, 1% antibiotic-antimycotic component, 1% sodium pyruvate, 1% l-glutamine, and 10% FBS. The cell lines were placed overnight in the incubator fixed with 37 °C and 5% CO2 supply. The biocompatibility and cell viability measurement were cross-checked using WST-1 assay. After in contact with the cell membranes, WST-1 molecule undergoes reduction pathways to yield dark-orange formazan. The absorbance at 450 or 600 nm were obtained to detect formazan using a microplate reader.

To examine the biocompatibility of CuCG nanocatalysts, first the HeLa, 4T1, and B16F1 cell lines were seeded (1×104 cells/well) in a 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h in the incubator. The cells were then washed twice with PBS before being cultured for 24 and 48 h in 100 μL medium containing CuCGC at different concentrations (0, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μg/mL). Before the analysis, the cells were cleansed with PBS to exclude non-internalized materials, and 10 μL of WST-1 reagent and 100 μL medium were administered to cells. After 90 min of incubation, the OD was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader. The cell viability was determined by comparing it to the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic efficacy of CuCGC nanocatalysts in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: In 96-well plate, B16F1, HeLa, and 4T1 cells were co-cultured with CuCG at various concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 200 ppm) for overnight. To activate gas therapy, the material was prepared in PBS at pH 5. For CDT evaluation, 15 μL of H2O2 (100 μM) was injected to the cells and incubated for 6 h. The cells were cultured in a medium containing 100 mM glucose to examine the starvation therapy. In vitro drug release was demonstrated by activating the •OH radical generation through CDT mechanism. Synergistic effects of CuCGC nanocatalysts was performed by introducing H2O2 and glucose solution into the cell lines at pH 5. Following that, 10 μL of WST-1 solution was added and placed in the incubator for 90 min. Subsequently, an OD was collected at 450 nm using a microplate reader. The viability of the cells was determined by comparing them to control cells. Chou and Martin's CompuSyn program (ComboSyn, Inc. Paramus, NJ 2007) was utilized to analyze the synergistic performance of enhanced CDT, glucose-guided ST, and chemotherapy (CT). The combination index (CI) value quantifies synergism (CI < 1), additive effects (CI = 1), and antagonism (CI > 1).

Initially, 4T1 cells (1.0×106 cell/well) were cultured in a 6-well plate for 24 h, followed by various treatments of CuCGC nanocatalyst-containing medium, and incubation for another 24 h, followed by apoptotic evaluation using FITC-annexin V (AV) and propidium iodide (PI) double staining cell solution (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA) for 30 min. The fluorescence intensity was then measured using a flow cytometer. The scatters in a plotted graph with two parameters were grouped into four quadrants. Lower right (LR) and upper right (UR) proportions reflected as early and late stages of the apoptotic process, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro bioimaging of CuCGC nanocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: For cellular uptake studies, 4T1 cancer cells were allowed to grow for overnight on glass slides embedded in a six-well plate. The cells were then washed twice with PBS before the additions of fresh medium diluted with various doses of CuCGC nanocatalysts. After incubating for 8 h, the cells were rinsed with PBS to eliminate remaining contaminants. Eventually, 2 mL of ethanol (75% purity) was poured into each well and fixed for 10 min before staining the cell nuclei with NucGreen as directed by the manufacturer. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (TCS SP2 equipment Leica, Germany) was used to examine the fluorescent images of the cells. Note that: blue, green, and red cellular images were obtained using 406, 488, and 561 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular ROS and H2S gas detection
ce:section.ce:para: DCFH-DA was utilized as the fluorescent probe to evaluate intracellular ROS formation. Briefly, 4T1 cells (1.0×106 per well) were seeded using 6-well plate for overnight before introducing a different ratio of CuCGC solution. After 6 h cultivation, the medium was discarded, and washed twice with PBS to eliminate remaining particles. Then, 10 μL of DCFH-DA was added to the medium and placed in the incubator for another 2 h. Afterward, 20 μL of glucose (50 mM) and H2O2 (100 μM) was added to well to activate the •OH radicals generations by CuCGC nanocatalysts. Eventually, fluorescence pictures of 4T1 cells were obtained using CLSM.

To assess the intracellular H2S content, 4T1 cells (1.0×106 per well) were treated for 6 h with CuCGC at varying doses (50 and 100 ppm). After 30 min, the cells were stained with Washington State Probe-5 (WSP-5, 100 μM) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 100 μM) and then rinsed in PBS. CLSM subsequently captured photographs of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative evaluation for CuCGC performance in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, HeLa and 4T1 cells were grown for overnight using six-well glass plate. The cells were then administered a predetermined quantity of CuCGC solution and incubated for 8 h. For CDT and GT assays, the cells were treated with 100 μM H2O2 and acidic PBS (pH = 5), respectively. The cells were fed 100 μM glucose for GOx-catalyzing starvation therapy. CuCGC was chemotherapeutically evaluated by treating the cells with 100 μM H2O2 and 100 μM glucose to trigger ROS production. The dead cells' membrane was then stained with 10 μL of PI solution (100 μM), and cellular photographs were collected using confocal microscopy.

To further understand the uptaking mechanism of CuCGC nanocatalysts, 4T1 cancer cells were treated in 10 μL of inhibitor solution for 1 h, including nystatin, amiloride hydrochloride, genistein, and Me-β-CD. The cells were then supplied with a fresh medium containing different amounts of CuCGC nanocatalysts and cultured for 3 h. To examine the temperature-dependent uptake mechanism, the cells were incubated with CuCGC material at three different temperatures: 4, 20, and 37 °C. Finally, the cellular picture was acquired using confocal microscopy.

The X-ray diﬀractometer (XRD Ultima IV Rigaku, USA 18 kW with rotating anode source Cu Kα1 line (λ = 1.54 Å) was used to identify the crystal structure of CQDs. Raman spectroscopy (MRS-320 Raman Instrument system (UniG2D, Republic of Korea) identified molecular interaction of CQDs by applying excitation wavelengths of 532 nm equipped with a microscope system. Fourier transformed infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) (model IR Tracer-100 (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to analyze the functional groups of CQDs with the wavenumber ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1 using potassium bromide (KBr) pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Escalab 250Xi spectrometer with Al-Kα radiation (hʋ = 1486.6 eV) determined the composition of CQDs. Ultraviolet/Visible-Photoluminescence spectrofluorometer (Horiba LTD., Japan) and UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1900) were used to measure fluorescence and absorbance spectra, respectively. The PLQYs of the as-prepared material were measured comparatively by referring to quinine sulfate (QS, PLQYs (54.6%)); the % PLQYs of Cu,N,S-CQDs were determined using Equation (5):

where I, A, and η are the integral PL intensity, UV absorbance and the optical density, and solvent reflective index, respectively. All data were obtained in triplicate, with the sample t-test (*p < 0.05,**p < 0.01,***p < 0.001,****p < 0.0001) on some data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical behavior of Cu,N,S-CQDs was studied using UV–visible and PL spectroscopy. Inset picture of Fig. 1e displays a bright blue solution for Cu,N,S-CQDs under UV lamp irradiation. Cu,N,S-CQDs showed two significant absorption peaks at 240 and 356 nm representing for π-π* electron transition of the conjugated ring, and n-π* electron transition of the carbonyl groups, respectively (Fig. 1e). Furthermore, Cu,N,S-CQDs exhibited high absorption maxima at 360 nm, suggesting that this wavelength might be employed as the excitation wavelength. By using 360 nm excitation wavelength, Cu,N,S-CQDs produced blue emission spectra centered at 443 nm, and full-width half-maximum (FWHM) was obtained as 71 nm. The stokes shift was calculated using λshift = λf max-λa max where f and a represents fluorescence and absorption maxima of the Cu,N,S-CQDs solution. Therefore, λshift of Cu,N,S-CQDs become 83 nm, indicating that fast relaxation from the initial state to the emissive state, could also be due to intramolecular energy-transfer. The PLQY of the Cu,N,S-CQDs solution was calculated using quinine sulfate (PLQY = 54.6%) reference solution, and found to be 84%. After being examined at different excitation wavelengths (320–450 nm), Fig. 1f clearly demonstrates that the as-synthesized Cu,N,S-CQDs have excitation-independent PL properties. Besides, the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescent mapping shown in Fig. 1g confirms that Cu,N,S-CQDs produced the most prominent peak near 440 nm at 360 nm of excitation wavelength, which is consistent with Fig. 1e–f.

Briefly, the optical intensity of CQDs was optimized and investigated by varying the reaction time, temperature, and precursor ratio. In accordance to the optimizations, when 5.2 mmol of citric acid was subjected to 200 °C for 10 min, it reached a maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3a). Following that, varying DETA ratio were introduced to pure CQDs, obtaining N-CQDs with a highest PL intensity at 2.0 mmol of DETA and then rapidly decreasing as the dopant ratio overflowed (Fig. S3b). The PLQYs of N-CQDs exceeded a maximum of 90.1% at a feeding ratio of 2.0 mmol DETA and dropped to around 60% at a feeding ratio of 10 mmol DETA. Meanwhile, after the addition of CuCl2 and Na2S precursors, the PL peak began to fall with increasing precursor concentration, as shown in Fig. S3c, due to the quenching effect of transition metal ions. The PLQYs of Cu,N,S-CQDs declined to about 84% when compared to the initial N-CQDs solution. Further, the PL lifetime investigation was conducted using time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) measurement as shown in Fig. 1h. In the analysis, the biexponential function (

. Cu,N-CQDs exhibit the shortest lifetime (8.227 ns) compared to N-CQDs (8.911 ns) and Cu,N,S-CQDs (11.063 ns). Cu,N,S-CQDs exhibit superior optical properties due to the elimination of non-radiative recombination and the formation of new radiative channel upon N and S doping, which subsequently enhances PL emission and suppress fluorescence decay of copper ion. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the chemical state and elemental compositions of Cu,N,S-CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. S6. The characteristics peaks for C, N, O, Cu, and S are found at 293, 403, 523, 945, and 171 eV binding energy, respectively, validating the successful preparation of Cu,N,S-CQDs. The atomic ratio of elements was estimated from XPS spectra with 41.27% C, 10.64% N, 41.95% O, 3.09% Cu, and 3.05% S, respectively, which is slightly different from the Cu,N-CQDs as proven by the Cu decrement due to sulfur doping (Table S2). The C 1s spectra for Cu,N-CQDs and Cu,N,S-CQDs were deconvoluted into multiple peaks corresponding to CC/C–C, CN/C–OH, CO, and O–CO, as shown in Fig. 2 a–b, where the spectra does not exhibit any notable change before and after sulfur injection. After that, three integrated signals corresponding to the pyridinic, pyrrolic, and quaternary N were obtained from the N 1s spectra of Cu,N-CQDs and Cu,N,S-CQDs (Fig. 2c–d), where the quaternary N content was reduced after sulfur doping and the pyrrolic N signal was amplified. The spectra of O 1s was split into three distinct peaks corresponding to C–O, C–OH, and O–CO, as depicted in Fig. 2e–f. The deconvoluted spectra of S 2p (Fig. 2g) in Cu,N,S-CQDs demonstrate that the predominant C–S–H/C–S–C groups emanated from S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 units, respectively, as well as a small fraction of SO2 and SO3 groups at 162.9, 164.4, 167.6, and 168.9 eV [43,44]. Cu 2p spectra of Cu,N-CQDs and Cu,N,S-CQDs revealed asymmetric peaks at 934.5–935.3 eV and 955.3–956.8 eV, corresponding to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, indicating that Cu (II) was doped in Cu,N-CQDs and Cu,N,S-CQDs (Fig. 2h–i). Furthermore, the satellite lines of Cu (II) were detected in the 937–950 eV range. The aforementioned findings conform the successfully integration of dopants ions into CQDs framework.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of CuCGC nanocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Combining a stimuli-responsive antibiotic with cascade enzymatic activity in a multifunctional anticancer agent would prevent the drawbacks of monotherapy and maximize drug release efficiency. Fig. 3 a presents the overall design and assembly of CuCGC nanocatalysts utilizing Cu,N,S-CQDs as nanocarrier. A stepwise recrystallization technique was followed to generate thiol linkers (TL), which were afterward connected with CPT using organic linkers to construct a ROS-sensitive TLCPT prodrug, as illustrated in Fig. 3a–b. The carboxyl group of Cu,N,S-CQDs was then activated using EDC/NHS-sulfo linkers, and TLCPT and GOx molecules were co-functionalized onto the surface of the nanocarrier. Material characterizations of CuCGC nanocatalysts was performed using electron dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), HRTEM, and FTIR techniques. Firstly, the elemental confirmation by EDS revealed that as-synthesized CuCGC is mainly consist of C, N, O, S, and Cu under Ni grid observation (Fig. S7). The HRTEM image of the CuCGC nanocatalysts revealed spherical morphology as shown in Fig. 3c, suggesting that GOx and CPT had no effect on the structural geometry. Due to the integrations of GOx and TLCPT, the average size of CuCGC was determined to be 5.28 ± 0.07 nm (Fig. 3d), which is slightly larger than Cu,N,S-CQDs (4.99 nm) as shown in Fig. 1a. Moreover, the lattice fringes of CuCGC of (200), as shown in Fig. 3e, were determined using the line profiles in Fig. 3f, indicating the crystalline nature of CuCGC, comparable to Cu,N,S-CQDs. Importantly, the FTIR observation in Fig. 3g verified that the CuCGC was successfully engineered. The peak at 1685 cm−1 in the CuCGC spectra confirms the stretching vibration of CO in amide linkage and has shifted from 1740 cm−1 due to the lower transmittance of carboxyl group contained in Cu,N,S-CQDs. The peak at 1657 cm−1 belongs to the aromatic group and validates the existence of TLCPT. The prominent peaks at 1410 and 1152 cm−1 are attributed to the C–N amide linkage and C–O–C stretching, respectively. The pronounced C–N band in the CuCGC spectrum supports that the GOx- and CPT-loading strategy was obtained through covalent interaction. In addition, the UV and PL spectra of CuCGC nanocatalysts before and after drug loading were studied. As shown in Fig. S8a, the PL intensity of CuCGC prepared by EDC/NHS linkers was better compared to the other approaches. More interestingly, the TR-PL data for CuCGC shows a longer lifetime (τavr = 11.140 ns), indicating the bright luminescent CuCGC product (Fig. S8c). UV–vis spectroscopy of the CuCGC nanocatalysts (Fig. 3h) showed the typical absorption band at 366 nm which is consistent with previous report [45], signifying that CPT was effectively loaded onto CuCG nanocarrier. Moreover, we used MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry (MS) to evaluate the covalent connection of TLCPT and GOx fragments in the CuCGC nanocatalysts. The MS signals of CuCGC shown in Fig. S8d, matched with the structural fragments of Cu,N,S-CQDs and CuCG, confirming that the final product was successfully constructed. The signal at 549 Da might correlate to the graphitic-like structure deposited within the carbon core [46]. According to Figs. S8e–S8i, the MS spectra of CuCGC nanocatalysts produce different signals at 582, 235, and 347 m/z identified as a TLCPT, TL, and CPT fragments, respectively, suggesting the effective inclusion of TLCPT on the CuCG surface. Additionally, zeta potential findings (Fig. 3i) revealed that Cu,N,S-CQDs was initially owned negative surface (−0.53 mV), which was contributed by hydroxyl, carboxyl, and some thiol groups. CuCG, on the other hand, exhibits positive charge following GOx functionalization and finally declines to −0.2 mV for CuCGC, suggesting effective TLCPT loading. The photostability of CuCGC was demonstrated in biological mediums for five days; nevertheless, the PL result (Fig. S9a) did not show any significant change after five days of incubation, showing that CuCGC nanocatalysts owned better stability in the dispersion medium. The storage capacity of CuCGC, was further demonstrated in Fig. S9b, claiming long-term dispersibility in different mediums, including distilled water (DIW), PBS, and DMEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu/pH-driven therapeutic studies of CuCGC nanocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The release of Cu2+ and S2− ions were preferable under suitable pH condition (pH = 5.0) to catalyze the formation of •OH radical and H2S gas for chemodynamic therapy (CDT) and gas therapy (GT) of cancer (Fig. S10a). Based on Fenton-like principles, Cu2+ ion was reduced to Cu + ion in the acidic condition and catalyze the decompositions of H2O2 to cytotoxic •OH radicals which can efficiently damage the cancer cells [47–51]. Further TEM research reveals the ionic disintegration of CuCGC structure after being treated with glucose and H2O2 solutions in a tumor microenvironment (TME). Figs. S11a and S11b represent those clear ionic dissociations occur at CuCGC, indicating the release of Cu2+ and S2− ions, CPT release, and gluconic acid formation after the ROS activation and glucose addition. Furthermore, the GSH presence in TME would interferent the stability of metal by converting it into reduced oxidation state, then destabilizing the material structure; thereby dissociating the nano-assembly structure [52].This result demonstrates that CuCGC is fully responsive under the TME circumstances. Fig. 4 a depicts typical UV spectra for oxTMB at 652 nm, indicating that when CuCGC was mixed with a sufficient amount of H2O2, it produced promising catalytic activity for •OH radical formation. Whereas, no significant absorption was observed at 652 nm when N-CQDs or TMB were used alone, confirming that the catalytic properties of CuCGC was Cu2+-dependent, supported in Figs. S10b–S10e. Furthermore, the concentration of oxTMB at 652 nm accelerated rapidly with increasing TMB (0–5 mM) and H2O2 (0–100 mM) concentration, signifies that a large amount of •OH radical was produced and that the catalytic performance of CuCGC is concentration dependent (Fig. 4b–c). The mixture displayed a strong blue color with escalating H2O2 and TMB concentration, as shown in Figs. S12a–S12b, which is consistent with UV spectral data. Kinetic models, such as the Michaelis-Menten (Km) and Lineweaver-Burk (Vmax) models, were employed to investigate the peroxidase-like activities of CuCGC by varying the ratio of TMB, H2O2, or glucose, as shown in Figs. S12c–S12f. Based on the results, the Vmax and Km values of CuCGC were identified to be 51.058×10−6 M/s and 176.7×10−6 M/s, respectively, noting that CuCGC attained excellent catalytic efficiency for •OH radical generation, and our findings were significantly better than previously reported papers (Table S3). Intracellular glutamine, which has antioxidant properties, reduces the efficacy of a ROS-based therapeutic platform by neutralizing radical species before they reach the tumor. Therefore, by inhibiting intracellular antioxidant activity, the ROS-based therapeutic performance of CuCGC could be improved. Cu2+ can effectively break down glutathione (GSH) molecule and form GSSG and Cu+ ion in an acidic environment, where 3,3′-dithio-bis(6-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) is used to detect GSSG and form yellowish solution indicating the formation of GS-TNB intermediate (Fig. 4d), which can be observed at 412 nm using a UV–vis spectroscopy [53]. Fig. 4e–f demonstrate the absorbance spectra of GSH mixed with CuCGC, with two significant peaks at 320 and 412 nm related to the GS-TNB and GSH concentration, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4g, the Cu2+ ion was reduced to Cu+ ion during the conversion of GSH to GSSG, which is beneficial for •OH radical generation by CuCGC. The CDT performance of CuCGC was thus trailed by adding a small amount of GSH solution, as shown in Fig. 4h, with the absorbance reading at 652 nm rapidly increasing, indicating that CuCGC nanocatalysts generated •OH radicals more efficiently than without GSH. Therefore, the CuCGC nanocatalysts demonstrated promising activity in terms of not only •OH radical generation but also antioxidant depletion for enhanced CDT of cancer disease.

Next, we evaluated the H2S gas production by CuCGC solution using methylene blue (MB) protocol for gas therapy (GT) of cancer. The acidic environment primarily facilitated the release of S2− ions, then combined with H+ ions to form toxic H2S gas, which was detected using a DMPD probe, and the resulting MB concentration was measured using UV spectroscopy at 663 nm [54,55]. After incubation with 200 ppm CuCGC (Fig. 4i) in mild acidic conditions, the formation of MB grown significantly and reached a maximum in the first 15 min, and then remained constant for the next 30 min, indicating fast H2S generation kinetics by CuCGC nanocatalysts. According to standard curve (Fig. S13), CuCGC produced 21.4 μM amount of H2S gas within 30 min, confirming potential candidate for gas-mediated cancer therapy. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4j, CuCGC exhibits concentration-dependent H2S gas production, where H2S formation rising exponentially and achieving an optimum value at 200 ppm of CuCGC. Similarly, the inset photograph in Fig. 4j validated that the color of the mixture gradually changed from colorless to red with increasing CuCGC concentration due to the presence of excess MB, confirming the concentration- and time-dependent production of H2S gas. For a qualitative examination of H2S generation, a piece of circular Whatman filter paper saturated with Pb(NO3)2 solution was used to assess the H2S gas generating capacity of CuCGC nanocatalysts [56–58]. When Pb2+ ion came into contact with H2S gas, it completely changed into PbS (equation (6)) and the paper turned black, which inferred the formation of H2S gas:
(6)
P
b
2 +
+
H 2
S
g
→
P b
S
s

Fig. 4k clearly demonstrates the Whatman paper turning black (at pH = 5) as the reaction time extends to 10 min, indicating the production of H2S gas. However, the test paper's color barely changed as the reaction time grew longer under normal pH conditions. In accordance with these findings, CuCGC could be used as a novel H2S gas generator for gas-mediated cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cascade enzymatic activity and stimuli-responsive drug release study
ce:section.ce:para: The covalently-linked GOx enables the CuCGC nanocatalysts to undergo an enzymatic reaction to produce gluconic acid and H2O2, as shown in Fig. 5 a, which can further promote self-supply enhanced CDT performance for tumor therapy. The enzymatic activity of GOx in CuCGC was determined using a typical calibration curve constructed with pure GOx solution (Fig. S14a). In the presence of GOx and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) enzymes, glucose oxidation and H2O2 breakdown occurred (Fig. S14b), respectively, which finally resulted in TMB oxidation. Except for Cu,N,S-CQDs (without GOx), the digital pictures (Figs. S14c–S14e) confirmed the emergence of deep yellowish color with increasing concentration, verifying glucose oxidation. As shown in Table S4, the enzymatic activity attains its maxima (18.8%) when 2 mg of GOx is used, indicating the effectivity of CuCGC nanocatalysts. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in Fig. S15 also shows successfully conjugated GOx in CuCGC nanocatalysts, as the weight loss reveals significant variation when compared to Cu,N,S-CQDs (before GOx loading). Further, we analyzed the formations of downstream products as illustrated in Fig. 5b–d. The H2O2 concentration was quantified utilizing of titanium sulfate (Ti(SO4)2); when reacting with H2O2 (Ti4+ + H2O2 + 2H2O → H2TiO4 + 4H+) it produced H2TiO4, which has a characteristic absorption peak at 405 nm. As displayed in Fig. 5b, upon using CuCGC nanocatalysts, the absorbance reading at 405 nm increased dramatically with increasing glucose concentration, indicating the formation of large amounts of H2O2. Similarly, free GOx, as a positive control, demonstrated higher H2O2 production properties, whereas a linear curve was obtained for Cu,N,S-CQDs, revealing no H2O2 production due to the absence of GOx. Likewise, the other downstream product, gluconic acid, was analyzed by the subsequent formation of the hydroxamate complex. Briefly, gluconic acid selectively react with hydroxylamine and produce an acid-hydroxamate complex, which is then followed by hydroxamate-Fe3+ complex solution (gluconic acid + hydroxylamine + Fe3+ → Hydroxamate-Fe3+) with an absorbance peak at 505 nm. Fig. 5c shows the relative absorption records at 505 nm in the presence of CuCGC and free GOx versus increased glucose levels, suggesting that CuCGC performed similarly to free GOx in accelerating the oxidation of glucose to yield gluconic acid. Similarly, as demonstrated in Fig. 5d–e, CuCGC and pure GOx exhibit a considerable reduction in pH value and dissolved O2 level. A large quantity of oxygen was utilized during the hydrolysis of glucose, and the lower pH value verified the generation of gluconic acid. Cu,N,S-CQDs revealed no substantial H2O2 and gluconic acid generation, a steady pH reading, and non-significant O2 consumption due to the absence of GOx. These findings confirmed that CuCGC nanocatalysts mimic remarkable enzymatic activity for glucose starvation therapy. Additionally, we investigated the •OH radical producing capability of CuCGC nanocatalysts by introducing concentration gradient of glucose solution, as shown in Fig. 5f, due to the formation of H2O2 through glucose oxidation process. The absorbance spectra of oxTMB at 652 nm were more pronounced as glucose frequency increases from 0 to 50 μM, confirming the activation of •OH radicals by self-supplying H2O2 via glucose metabolism. Figs. S16a–S16b reveals Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk curves, with Vmax of l.214×10−6 M/s and possessed excellent catalyzing efficiency towards TMB oxidation.

CuCGC nanocatalysts exhibited the highest CPT-loading efficiency of about 81.3% as displayed in Fig. S8b, referred by standard curve (Fig. S17a). The chemistry of drug release from nanocarriers has the potential to significantly influence the anticancer efficacy of the biomaterial. For an effective chemotherapeutic response, the drug should be readily cleavable and able to diffuse to the nucleus structure upon activation by particular external or internal stimuli. Thus, we developed ROS-responsive nanocarrier by taking advantages of •OH radical generating properties of CQDs and ROS-sensitive TLCPT prodrug. Fig. 5g presents cumulative drug releasing properties of CuCGC at different conditions. No significant release was occurring at pH = 7 and only 13% of CPT release was observed, as shown in Fig. S17b. Subsequently, the released CPT showed a significantly increased to 22.5% after the pH tuned 5.5, proving that CuCGC demonstrate pH-dependent drug release. The experiment was conducted by introducing 100 μM H2O2, resulting the drug release was accelerated and reached to 62% due to formation of •OH radical via Fenton-reaction. In the presence of 100 μM glucose, the releasing performance was found as 53%, which is comparable result with H2O2 case. As demonstrated in Fig. 5g, CuCGC nanocatalysts produce •OH radical when combining with H2O2 and/or glucose through Fenton-reaction and starvation-guided-Fenton-reaction, which can effectively damage the TL bridges and easily detach CPT molecules from the nanocarrier. When combining H2O2 and glucose together, the releasing concentration attains up to 66%, due to the enhanced ROS generation via the synergistic pathway. Subsequently, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were applied to investigate the production of ·OH utilizing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as an ·OH trap. After CuCGC treatment in mildly acidic, glucose, and H2O2-assisted treatments, a distinctive ·OH signal with a ratio of 1:2:2:1 appeared in Fig. 5h. These data strongly suggest an efficient enzymatic approach for ROS generation in TME. Based on these outcomes, the TL linkage was quickly cleaved under radical exposure, as demonstrated in Fig. 5i and reached to maximum releasing efficacy for enhanced drug-mediated cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and therapeutic efficacy of CuCGC nanocatalysts in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability and intracellular effectiveness of CuCGC nanocatalysts were further illustrated using in vitro observation using the HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 cell lines. Various doses of aqueous CuCGC solution were incubated with HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 cell lines at specific time intervals, and cell viability was determined using the WST-1 assay, as shown in Fig. 6 a–c. The cell viabilities of HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 remain unchanged after 48 h incubation with increasing CuCG doses up to 500 ppm, exemplifying that the covalent interaction of the CQDs prevents the release of copper ions into the cell structure and improves biocompatibility [59]. Additionally, the initial Cu,N,S-CQDs reveals low toxicity towards HeLa and 4T1 cancer cells after three days incubation as shown in Figs. S18a–S18b. It is proven that CuCG material exhibited excellent biocompatibility and safer to apply in vitro studies. Further investigation using 50% cytotoxic concentration (CC50) revealed that the CuCG concentration required to induce 50% reduction in living cells in HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 cells are 922, 14816, and 2685 μg/mL, respectively (Figs. S18c–S18e). Based on the CC50 value, we can conclude that CuCG nanocatalysts owned lesser toxicity and could be a viable alternative for anticancer therapy. CuCGC-induced intracellular ROS formation was investigated using a colorless DCFH-DA probe molecule. DCFH-DA was first hydrolyzed into DCFH by intracellular esterase, then reacted with ROS to produce DCF intermediates that emit a green light, as illustrated in Fig. S19a [60]. Fig. 6d shows confocal images of 4T1 cells incubated with varying concentrations of CuCGC and H2O2, or glucose activator. Without H2O2 or glucose, group 1 generates non-emissive images, indicating the absence of ROS. However, after adding glucose activator, the image in group 2 turned green due to the formation of ROS via GOx-catalyzed enzymatic activity. The green emission grew increasingly as the CuCGC concentration enhanced, indicating that enzymatic reaction is concentration dependent. Likewise, after incubating the cells with H2O2, green spots established (group 3), implying the production of •OH radicals via Cu2+-mediated Fenton-reaction. With increasing CuCGC doses, the phenomenon became more pronounced (group 4). Notably, the synergistic effects of H2O2 and glucose were demonstrated as shown in group 5, with ultra-fluorescent green cellular images denoting those huge quantities of ROS were produced intracellularly by CuCGC nanocatalysts. This finding was further supported using HeLa cancer cells as shown in Fig. S19b. The green cellular images were observed after incubating the cells with H2O2 and/or glucose. Collectively, as shown in Figs. S19c–S19d, a strong green fluorescence signal was observed in response to all exogenous stimuli, indicating multi-stimuli activated ROS generation. The results indicate that, in addition to the H2O2-activated CDT effect, CuCGC nanocatalysts effectively deplete glucose and convert it to •OH radicals without the addition of H2O2, which would be beneficial for tumor starvation therapy-triggered CDT. The cellular uptake process of biomaterial should be well-defined for effective in vitro clinical benefit. We employed a Washington State Probe-5 (WSP-5) H2S fluorescent probe to further monitor H2S gas generated by 4T1 cells in an in vitro study. In this case, the fluorescent base is 2-pyridyl disulfide, which initiates fluorescence through a nucleophilic substitution-cyclization reaction that liberates the fluorophores. It has been demonstrated that the WSP-5 probe may promptly and selectively produce a green fluorescence signal when interacting with H2S in cells [61]. A higher CuCGC nanocatalyst concentration increased the fluorescence of WSP-5 (depicted in Fig. 6e). This result was also found in the Na2S group, indicating that CuCGC can stimulate the release of H2S gas from within cells. Fig. 6f depicts endocytosis pathway studies for CuCGC nanocatalysts employing various protein-based inhibitors such as nystatin, genistein, amiloride, and methyl-β-cyclodextrin (Me-β-CD). Comparable fluorescence images were obtained for amiloride and Me-β-CD inhibitors, whereas the brightness of the cellular images became completely non-luminescent when nystatin and genistein inhibitors were used, indicating that the cellular receptors were blocked by the inhibitors and no CuCGC was absorbed by the cells. Thus, caveolin-mediated and caveolin-mediated endocytosis routes are the most likely mechanisms for effective absorption of CuCGC nanocatalysts by cancer cells (Fig. 6g). We also investigated at the temperature dependence on cellular uptaking of CuCGC. As shown in Fig. 6h, the fluorescence images of the cells declined significantly when the incubation temperature was reduced to 20 and 4 °C, respectively, as compared to the control experiment, which was done at 37 °C. These findings confirm that the overall cellular uptake mechanism was temperature-based caveolin-mediated endocytosis pathways. In addition, we acquired z-stacking fluorescent pictures of 4T1 cells to support the feasibility of intracellular uptake. Fig. S20 illustrates the fluorescence ability of 4T1 cells gradually improving from Z1 (top surface) to Z6 (center), where the emission achieves its maxima. And the bottom surface (Z7-Z12) exhibited poor luminescence, showing that CuCGC typically accumulated around the nucleus structure, which is beneficial for high nucleus-targeted therapeutic efficacy against cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro synergetic performance of CuCGC nanocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Besides that, the in vitro synergistic output of CuCGC nanocatalysts against cancer cells was appraised by activating exogenous stimuli such as H2O2 and glucose, as depicted in Fig. 7 a–c. The viability of HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 cells was slightly reduced with increasing CuCGC nanocatalysts and finally dropped to 90, 83, and 76%, respectively, expected to be due to the unconditional CPT activation which leads to cell damage. In the same way, after fixing the pH at 5, the living cell percentage gradually decline with the optimum CuCGC doses owing to the generation of toxic H2S gas and pH-activated CPT release. The release of S2− ions was preferred in an acidic pH condition compared to a normal pH, and combined with H+ ions to form H2S gas, which can quickly damage a cell's mitochondria. According to the results, 64, 62, and 41% of cell death was recorded for HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 cells, correspondingly, attributed to the GT and CT effects of CuCGC. We also analyzed the CDT efficacy of CuCGC by introducing small amount of H2O2 solution. The cell reduction was rapidly increase with enhancing CuCGC ratio and reaches to 55, 64, and 77% due to activation of ROS, H2S gas and ROS/pH-driven CPT release. After incubation with H2O2 solution, CuCGC undergo set-of-reaction to produce toxic •OH radicals via Cu2+-mediated Fenton-activity for ROS-activated cancer therapy. The synergistic effects of CDT, GT, CT, and ST mechanism were also proven using exogenous supply of H2O2 and glucose at acidic condition, causing rapid cell death with increasing CuCGC concentration. The cell viability dropped significantly with increasing material molar ratio; thus, at 200 ppm of CuCGC, only 30, 29, and 20% of, owing to the release of ROS and H2S gas, as well as ROS/pH-mediated CPT release, and through GOx-catalyzed glucose oxidation. Fig. 7d–f clearly summarize the therapeutic-killing efficiency of CuCGC (at 200 ppm) nanocatalysts against HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 cells, respectively, via different mechanisms. In accordance with the figures, combined therapeutic approaches such as CDT, CT, ST, and GT result in massive cellular damage with about 70, 71, and 80% of cell death occurring in HeLa, B16F1, and 4T1 cells, respectively. Cu2+ and S2− ions were released from CuCGC to the tumor tissue at lower pH condition, intensifying the formation of harmful •OH radicals and H2S gas, resulting in disabling mitochondrial activity and cell damage. Furthermore, GOx-catalyzed the destruction of cell nutrient, which stimulates oxidation therapy, leads to cancer cell clearance due to the starving effect. The oxidation of glucose promotes H2O2 production, which amplifies the CDT effect via self-H2O2 supply. Intracellular gluconic acid formation brings down the pH level, which then promotes S2− and Cu2+ release for H2S gas and •OH catalyzing reaction for enhanced GT and CDT efficacy, respectively. In addition, H2O2 and glucose activation not only improve CDT performance but also trigger CPT release from CuCGC for cancer chemotherapy. The production of •OH radicals cleave the TL bond and facilitates CPT detachment, inducing cell clearance via DNA binding and malfunctioning.

Comparing the relative cell viability of each treatment group to that of the control group revealed the synergistic performance of CuCGC nanocatalyst after 24 h of incubation at the same concentration (200 μg/mL) of CuCGC. Moreover, CuCGC exhibited the highest cell-killing efficiency (80% of cell death) towards 4T1 cells (Fig. 7f). As shown in Fig. 7d–f, CuCGC, at 200 μg/mL, induced cell toxicity under glucose addition and exhibited increased cell inhibition with an increase in concentration. Based on its enzymatic cascade activity, these findings show that the CuCGC nanocatalyst may produce toxic ROS that impedes cell proliferation. This nanocatalyst reveals that the combination index equals 0.372, compared with the camptothecin drug (Fig. S21). The nanocatalyst can consume glucose, thereby starving cancer cells in the tumor site. As a result, these findings confirm that CuCGC provides more effective cancer ablation than single therapy based on their synergistic effect of ST, CDT, GT, and CT.

Annexin V-FITC-PI flow cytometric analysis of 4T1 cells performed consistent results of the apoptotic cell induced by CuCGC with varying treatments quantitatively (Fig. 7g). Slight cell apoptosis was observed (16.3%) after CT and GT activation. Compared with other groups, CT + GT showed more necrosis, indicating that H2S gas release exhibited an intense effect on limiting cell proliferation [62]. After being triggered by H2O2 (CT + GT + CDT) and glucose (CT + GT + ST + CDT), the apoptosis ratio multiplied to 25.2% and 41.0%, respectively, signifying the activation of synergically ST and CDT on cancer cell mortality. The potential of using CuCGC nanocatalysts in cancer therapy was eventually quantified using PI staining. HeLa and 4T1 cells were treated with H2O2 and/or glucose after being incubated with 200 ppm CuCGC for 8 h, and confocal images of dead cells stained with PI solution were obtained as shown in Fig. 7h–i. The nuclei of the cancer cells were labeled with NucGreen for cellular monitoring [63,64], emitting bright green luminescent as revealed by the confocal photographs in Fig. 7h–i; the strong blue cellular images indicated CuCGC nanocatalysts localization not only in the cytoplasm but also in the cell nucleoli, according to the merge group of Fig. 7h–i. However, due to the absence of H2O2 and glucose (group 1), the PI-channel images do not produce red-luminescent, indicating no cell death. After activating the pH at 5, a significant number of red-spots were observed, owing to H2S production and subsequent cell damage. We also tested the combined effects of CT and ST by introducing glucose, which resulted in a high number of red spots due to starvation-guided ROS generation and ROS-cleavable CPT release for cancer therapy. Similarly, when using H2O2 solution, a huge proportion of red cells were obtained due to the activated ROS and ROS-induced CPT, causing massive cell damage. The synergistic performance of CuCGC was analyzed by combining H2O2 and glucose, which demonstrated bright red emission attributed to stimuli-responsive CDT, ST, CT and GT mechanism. Overall, this investigation demonstrates the potential of CuCGC nanocatalyst to act as chemotherapy/chemodynamic and starvation-inducing agents for collective cancer therapy via systematic in vitro studies at the intracellular level. We anticipate that CuCGC nanocatalyst might provide a potential therapeutic candidate in future clinical application.

In summary, we successfully modulated a multifunctional CuCGC nanocatalysts by co-functionalization of Cu,N,S-CQDs with GOx and CPT molecules for efficient cancer therapy. CuCGC demonstrated remarkable ROS generating performance in the presence of H2O2 and glucose activator through Fenton-mechanism and ST-induced Fenton reaction. Moreover, the CuCGC catalyzes the breakdown of glucose nutrient and toxic H2S gas production for starvation and gas therapy, respectively. The kinetic studies approve that CuCGC possessed high selectivity towards •OH generation activity. Integrations of CDT-driving agent with GOx enzyme in one platform results with enhanced ROS generation performance of CuCGC for effective cancer therapy. ROS-cleavable drug release was conducted for CuCGC in the presence of H2O2 and glucose, showing about 66% of CPT was released. The material exhibited better biocompatibility and stability in various biological medium. Moreover, upon the introduction of exogenous stimuli (H2O2 and glucose), CuCGC produced significant cells clearance via CDT/ST-driven chemotherapy and GT mechanisms. The in vitro cellular investigations and aqueous-dispersibility of CuCGC nanocatalysts support the potential future application in vivo clinical analysis.

XRD and XPS spectra for N-CQDs, Cu,N-CQDs, and Cu,N,S-CQDs. Mass spectra for CuCG and CuCGC. Photo-colloidal stability of CuCGC under different biological media. Comparison of the rate of TMB oxidation using CuCGC and other reported materials summarized in Table. Cell toxicity of Cu,N,S-CQDs as well as CC50 value og CuCG.
